summary: The post explains Capital Appreciation Bonds (CABs), a municipal financing instrument that allows towns to borrow money now while deferring all payments for 30-40 years, resulting in payouts 7-20 times the original loan. O'Neil highlights how CABs exploit an accounting loophole where only the original loan amount appears on balance sheets as a liability, not the eventual payout, allowing desperate or corrupt town officials to circumvent debt limits. She uses California school districts as examples, where $190 million borrowed will cost $1 billion when due. The post situates this within a broader critique of Wall Street's exploitation of unsophisticated local governments.
key_points: CABs are zero-coupon bonds with very long terms (30-40 years) where municipalities pay nothing until maturity, then owe massive sums due to compound interest
A key accounting loophole allows towns to record only the original loan amount as debt, not the eventual payout, helping them evade debt limits
The power imbalance between sophisticated Wall Street banks and 'temporary, ignorant, and desperate' local officials creates a toxic environment for municipal financing
Some states like Michigan and Ohio have banned CABs, while California is attempting restrictions, but bank lobbying creates 'serious headwinds'
The structure incentivizes short-term thinking since officials who approve these bonds won't be in office—and might be dead—when the bill comes due
money_quotes: If you add into that mix the fact that local town officials are often temporary, ignorant, and desperate, we get a toxic environment.
200 California school and community college districts issuing these bonds will end up paying 10 to 20 times more than they borrowed.
If the cash flows are the same for two different kinds of bonds, how do you get to account for them differently?

CABs are very attractive indeed, because it looks kind of innocuous and their overall debt limits don't get breached even though they've essentially sold the future of the town to a big Wall Street bank.
Plus they won't be in office when that bill comes due, and they might well be dead.
Given how much money they make for big banks, there are serious headwinds for reasonable rules.
key_insight: Capital Appreciation Bonds exploit an accounting loophole that lets municipalities hide their true debt burden, effectively allowing current officials to mortgage their town's future for short-term relief while Wall Street profits enormously.
